segments.text: PART 3
MAYA: So Finn, I've done as much as I can for our project on theatre programmes. How's your research coming along?
segments.translation: 玛雅：那么，芬恩，我已经尽我所能为我们的戏剧节目项目做了一些工作。你的研究进展如何？

segments.text: FINN: OK, MAYA. I didn't know theatre programmes are called playbills in the USA till I started looking into the topic.
segments.translation: 芬恩：好的，玛雅。在我开始研究这个主题之前，我并不知道戏剧节目在美国被称为剧单。

segments.text: Even though I struggled to find many useful websites, I'm glad we picked this subject.
segments.translation: 尽管我费了很大劲才找到许多有用的网站，但我很高兴我们选择了这个主题。

segments.text: No one else on the course is doing the same as us, although it is one of the research areas of the module convenor.
segments.translation: 课程中没有其他人在做与我们相同的事情，尽管这是模块召集者的研究领域之一。

segments.text: MAYA: That might actually put some people off.
segments.translation: 玛雅：这可能会让一些人反感。

segments.text: FINN: I suppose so. Anyway, I hadn't realised there are actually companies specialising in creating theatre programmes.
segments.translation: 芬恩：我想是的。不管怎样，我之前没有意识到实际上有专门负责制作戏剧节目的公司。

segments.text: MAYA: Yes, they're quite common nowadays.
segments.translation: 玛雅：是的，如今这种情况相当普遍。

segments.text: Contrary to what many people think, theatres don't hire people to do the programmes, in fact companies buy the rights to publish programmes on the theatre's behalf – and then make their money selling advertising space within the programme booklet.
segments.translation: 与许多人的想法相反，剧院并不雇人制作节目，事实上，公司购买了代表剧院发布节目的权利 —— 然后通过出售节目手册中的广告空间来赚钱。

segments.text: FINN: It must be easier for theatres to do it that way.
segments.translation: 芬恩：对剧院来说，这样做肯定更容易。

segments.text: MAYA: Yes.
segments.translation: 玛雅：是的。

segments.text: FINN: I remember reading something about programmes in early British theatre.
segments.translation: 芬恩：我记得读过一些关于英国早期戏剧节目的内容。

segments.text: It said that the cast was always very important.
segments.translation: 据说演员阵容始终非常重要。

segments.text: MAYA: Yeah, audiences were very familiar with leading actors, and big names would draw huge crowds.
segments.translation: 玛雅：是的，观众对主要演员非常熟悉，而大名人会吸引大量观众。

segments.text: FINN: But I hadn't realised that if the programme named a famous actor, that's who the public expected to perform.
segments.translation: 芬恩：但我当时没有意识到，如果节目提名了一位著名演员，那就是观众期待他表演的人。

segments.text: And if that didn't happen, people accused the theatre of breaking their agreement with the audience.
segments.translation: 如果这种情况没有发生，人们就会指责剧院违反了与观众的协议。

segments.text: They would demand refunds, and, if they didn't get them, there were riots!
segments.translation: 他们会要求退款，如果得不到退款，就会引发骚乱！

segments.text: MAYA: Outrageous. That'd never happen now.
segments.translation: 玛雅：太过分了。这种事现在绝对不会发生了。

segments.text: FINN: No, people are too polite, even when they're disappointed if the star of the show misses a performance.
segments.translation: 芬恩：不，人们太客气了，即使当节目明星错过一场表演时，他们也会感到失望。

segments.text: MAYA: We should definitely include that information about early audiences in our project.
segments.translation: 玛雅：我们绝对应该在我们的项目中包含这些关于早期观众的信息。

segments.text: I also think it's important to mention that lots of ordinary people at that time were illiterate, so theatre programmes were of limited value in advertising plays.
segments.translation: 我还认为值得提及的是，当时许多普通人都是文盲，因此戏剧节目在广告剧中的价值有限。

segments.text: When a company of actors arrived in a town, they'd parade around the streets in their costumes, beating drums and announcing their upcoming performances.
segments.translation: 当一群演员到达一个城镇时，他们会穿着戏服在街上游行，敲着鼓，宣布即将到来的演出。

segments.text: FINN: Interesting. I couldn't imagine that happening now either.
segments.translation: 芬恩：有意思。我也无法想象这种事现在还会发生。

segments.text: MAYA: There's also an interesting comparison to make between eighteenth and nineteenth-century programmes.
segments.translation: 玛雅：18 世纪和 19 世纪的节目之间也有一个有趣的比较。

segments.text: FINN: Wasn't it in the nineteenth century that theatre programmes started to resemble programmes today?
segments.translation: 芬恩：难道不是在 19 世纪，戏剧节目才开始像今天的节目吗？

segments.text: MAYA: Yes. And, unlike programmes from the eighteenth century, they always used colour.
segments.translation: 玛雅：是的。而且，与 18 世纪的节目不同，它们总是使用色彩。

segments.text: FINN: And there was a greater variety of designs.
segments.translation: 芬恩：而且设计的种类更多样化。

segments.text: But personally, I think eighteenth-century programmes were superior because they told the theatre-goers so many things, including about the actors.
segments.translation: 但就我个人而言，我认为 18 世纪的节目更优秀，因为它们向观众传达了许多信息，包括演员的情况。

segments.text: MAYA: And about the writer, the plot, and sometimes the history of the play.
segments.translation: 玛雅：还有关于作家、情节，有时甚至是戏剧的历史。

segments.text: FINN: That's right… What should we say about theatre programmes in the twentieth century?
segments.translation: 芬恩：没错...... 那么我们该如何评价 20 世纪的戏剧节目呢？

segments.text: MAYA: I reckon the most important thing is the dramatic change they underwent during World War Two.
segments.translation: 玛雅：我认为最重要的是它们在第二次世界大战期间经历的戏剧性变化。

segments.text: FINN: When the government imposed restrictions on the use of paper.
segments.translation: 芬恩：当时政府对纸张的使用施加了限制。

segments.text: MAYA: Yeah, but that was only in the UK. In the USA, programmes – or rather, playbills – continued to be published in the same format ...
segments.translation: 玛雅：是的，但那只是在英国。在美国，程序 —— 或者更确切地说，剧本 —— 继续以相同的格式发布......

segments.text: FINN: … while here in the UK, programmes became merely a single sheet of paper folded to create four pages for text.
segments.translation: 芬恩：...... 而在英国，节目单变成了一张对折的纸，形成了四页的文本。

segments.text: MAYA: What I don't really get is that, after the war, they didn't go back to being more than one sheet, or change in any way for over twenty-five years.
segments.translation: 玛雅：我真正不明白的是，战争结束后，它们在超过二十五年的时间里没有回到多于一页的状态，也没有发生任何变化。

segments.text: I know there were paper shortages after the war, but only for five or ten years.
segments.translation: 我知道战后确实出现了纸张短缺，但只持续了五到十年。

segments.text: FINN: Strange.
segments.translation: 芬恩：奇怪。

segments.text: MAYA: I've got some pictures of programmes we could include on the slides for our presentation.
segments.translation: 玛雅：我有一些节目的照片，可以放在我们演讲的幻灯片上。

segments.text: FINN: I found a couple too, MAYA. Let's go through and see what we think.
segments.translation: 芬恩：我也发现了几张，玛雅。让我们来看看，看看我们的想法。

segments.text: MAYA: Erm …, this is an old one, for a play called Ruy Blas.
segments.translation: 玛雅：嗯...... 这是一个老节目，为一部名叫《鲁伊・布拉斯》的戏剧制作的。

segments.text: FINN: Never heard of that, but the programme looks very decorative.
segments.translation: 芬恩：从没听说过，但这个节目看起来很有装饰性。

segments.text: MAYA: Good enough to put in a frame on the wall. The images are just beautiful. FINN, what did you find?
segments.translation: 玛雅：好到可以在墙上挂个相框了。画面真的很漂亮。芬恩，你发现了什么？

segments.text: FINN: I've got some pages from a programme for Man of La Mancha.
segments.translation: 芬恩：我找到了几页《梦幻骑士》节目单。

segments.text: I thought this was a good programme to show, not because of the pictures, but because it contains articles written by members of the theatre company.
segments.translation: 我认为这是一个很好的节目单，不是因为图片，而是因为它包含了剧团成员写的文章。

segments.text: So we can learn how the production was created, and the thoughts and feelings of the cast.
segments.translation: 这样我们就能了解这部作品是如何创作的，以及演员们的想法和感受。

segments.text: MAYA: Good. I've got a copy of a programme that's now in a museum.
segments.translation: 玛雅：很好。我有一份现在收藏在博物馆里的节目单副本。

segments.text: It's for The Tragedy of Jane Shore and it's said to be the earliest surviving document to have been printed on Australia's first printing press.
segments.translation: 这是为《简・肖尔的悲剧》准备的，据说它是澳大利亚第一台印刷机印刷的最早现存文件。

segments.text: FINN: Fantastic.
segments.translation: 芬恩：太棒了。

segments.text: MAYA: Another programme to talk about is for The Sailors' Festival.
segments.translation: 玛雅：另一个值得一提的节目是水手节。

segments.text: It comes from the British Library's digitised collection of programmes that was started a few years ago.
segments.translation: 它来自大英图书馆几年前开始收藏的数字化节目集。

segments.text: It already comprises over two hundred thousand programmes, which is amazing.
segments.translation: 它已经包含了超过二十万个节目，这真是令人惊叹。

segments.text: FINN: Wish I'd known about it while I was doing my research!
segments.translation: 芬恩：真希望我在做研究的时候就知道这个！
